title: A $1.8bn injection to boost health
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 WNews A $1.8bn injection to boost health Daily Telegraph EXCLUSIVE Madeleine Bower 3 May 2026 495 words English DAITEL 5 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Anthony Albanese will lock in $1.8bn in this month’s budget to make Medicare Urgent Care Clinics permanent, in the hope it will relieve pressure on emergency departments. The cash injection is set to be the biggest new health spend in the federal budget, locking in funding for the 137 urgent care clinics in perpetuity. The entire program costs more than $525.6m per year to run, with that amount to be in future budgets forever from 2030-31. The $1.8bn will top up existing provisional funding to run the clinics over the next five years. Prior to this, the funding was based on a trial system, with the original investment slated to finish in June this year. The Prime Minister has framed making urgent care clinics permanent as one of the government’s signature cost-of-living measures, as the clinics are 100 per cent bulk-billed. Mr Albanese said since the first urgent care clinics were introduced in June 2023, the flow-on effects were now being felt by hospitals. “Our number one priority is delivering cost-of-living relief for Australians – our network of Medicare urgent care clinics is doing exactly that,” he said. “These clinics are good for wallets, good for health, and are taking pressure off local emergency departments.” The government has recorded nearly three million presentations across the country’s urgent care clinic network since its inception, including more than 623,000 in NSW, yet there is no proof the measures have reduced hospital ED wait times. In the latest independent report into the efficacy of urgent care clinics, finalised in December last year, it estimated the program had reduced equivalent hospital ED presentations by between 4.6 and 10 per cent nationally. Additionally, it reported that, between June 2023 and August 2025, about 45 per cent of patients who presented to urgent care clinics said that without the service they would have gone to a hospital ED or called an ambulance. However, the report noted that despite the estimated reduction in ED presentations, there is still “no clear evidence that waiting times and the proportion of patients seen on time has changed.” According to the latest Bureau of Health Information data for the final quarter of 2025, 66.3 per cent of patients started their treatment on time while in NSW hospital EDs. This figure was relatively stable from the previous year. However, the overall number of ED attendees increased by 2.2 per cent from the previous year. The interim report also found that while the clinics were more cost effective than hospitals, many were struggling to fill workforce shortages and as a result were often unable to provide after-hours access to X-ray and pathology services as originally promised. Health Minister Mark Butler said the $1.8bn meant that urgent care clinics were “here to stay”. The Telegraph-20260503-Telegraph-5-000500627795 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Australian government will allocate $1.8 billion in the upcoming budget to make Medicare Urgent Care Clinics permanent, aiming to alleviate pressure on emergency departments. This funding will ensure the ongoing operation of 137 clinics, which have recorded nearly three million presentations since their introduction in June 2023. Despite some evidence of reduced hospital emergency department presentations, there is no clear indication that wait times have improved.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: $1.8 billion funding to make urgent care clinics permanent
Clinics have seen nearly three million presentations since June 2023
No clear evidence of reduced emergency department wait times despite reduced presentations
relevance: This funding initiative impacts healthcare costs and access, which are critical considerations for actuaries involved in health insurance and public policy analysis.
